name: Anandabodha
dates_notes: 11th c. consensus per Hiriyanna, Dasgupta, and Potter (Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies vol. III): immediately following Vimuktātman; pre-Citsukha by quotation. Standard placement is c. 1050–1100.
core_thesis: Ānandabodha consolidates Vivaraṇa-Advaita into a polemical-epistemological idiom. Earlier Advaitins had defended their central claims chiefly by *śruti*; Ānandabodha turns instead to formal *anumāna* (inference). He inherits the Vivaraṇa-line *brahmāśrita-bhāva-rūpā-avidyā* doctrine (developed earlier in Vimuktātman and Sarvajñātman) and the *cidābhāsa* analysis of the *jīva*, and turns the technical apparatus outward against the *bhedavāda* opponents: Naiyāyikas, Mīmāṃsakas, and the rising Bhedābheda and proto-Dvaita schools. His signature is the standard *mithyātva-anumānas* — most prominently the *dṛśyatvānumāna* (whatever is *dṛśya* is *mithyā*, like the silver superimposed on shell) and the *jaḍatvānumāna* — by which the *anirvacanīya-mithyātva* of the world is demonstrated. *Mithyā* is throughout glossed as *sad-asad-vilakṣaṇa*, neither real nor unreal; it never collapses into *asat*. He further defends Brahman as *svaprakāśa*, self-luminous, on the model of the *svayaṃ-prakāśatva* of *cit*. Citsukha's *Tattva-Pradīpikā* is a deepening and tightening of this programme; the same *anumānas* re-emerge two centuries later in the *Advaita-Siddhi* as the polemical core Vyāsatīrtha must dismantle.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Makaranda
engaged_works.summary: Compact polemical *prakaraṇa* whose title — 'flower-cluster (*makaranda*) of reasoned argument (*nyāya*)' — announces the method. The Advaita theses are established by formal inference. Ānandabodha defends the *svayaṃ-prakāśatva* of Brahman / *ātman*; refutes the Nyāya position that cognition is known by a further inference (*jñāna-jñāne anumeyatva*); establishes *anirvacanīya-mithyātva* by the *dṛśyatvānumāna*, on the standard analogy of the silver superimposed on shell (*śukti-rajata*); and refutes *bheda*, real difference, as itself analytically indeterminable. The treatise is one of the principal sources for Citsukha's *Tattva-Pradīpikā* and for the inferential machinery of the *Advaita-Siddhi*. It matters as the first Advaita work argued primarily in the technical idiom of *pramāṇa-vāda* rather than by exegesis of *śruti*.

engaged_works.title: Pramana-Mala
engaged_works.summary: Treatise — 'garland (*mālā*) of the *pramāṇas*' — that examines and accepts the six *pramāṇas* of the Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā (*pratyakṣa, anumāna, upamāna, śabda, arthāpatti, anupalabdhi*) for transactional purposes, while subordinating them all to *śabda* in the form of the Mahāvākya as the unique *brahma-pramāṇa*. The expository contribution is the careful demarcation of the architecture later canonised by Dharmarāja's *Vedānta-Paribhāṣā*. Each *pramāṇa* is *vyāvahārika*, valid until *bādha*. Only *śabda* qua Mahāvākya generates the *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* whose function is to remove avidyā. The work also extends the *mithyātva-anumānas* of the *Nyāya-Makaranda* and supplies refinements that recur in Citsukha and Madhusūdana.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Dipavali
engaged_works.summary: 'Row of lamps (*dīpāvalī*) of reasoned argument' — a polemical *prakaraṇa* in which Ānandabodha sharpens the inferential program of the *Nyāya-Makaranda* with explicit attention to opponents' counter-positions. The *dṛśyatva* and *jaḍatva* inferences for *mithyātva* are rehearsed and tightened. *Bhāva-rūpā-avidyā* is defended against the Naiyāyika view that ignorance is mere absence (*abhāva*). The *svayaṃ-prakāśatva* inference for *cit* is refined. Standard objections — the regress of cognitions, the locus problem for avidyā, the apparent reality of *bheda* — are worked through with formalised *anumāna*-structures. The work matters as a bridge text. Many of the *Advaita-Siddhi*'s opening *anumānas* are recognisably refinements of the *Dīpāvalī* line, taken up first by Citsukha and then by Madhusūdana.
